title: Why Consumer Choice Models Beat Gut Instinct Every Time
body:
When a global FMCG brand came to me convinced their packaging redesign would lift sales by 30%, the data told a very different story. Their marketing team had run focus groups, gathered enthusiastic feedback, and built a confident business case. But choice modelling — simulating how real shoppers trade off between competing options — revealed the redesign would likely cannibalise their premium SKU and yield a net share loss of 4%.

They launched anyway. Six months later, they were back asking why the numbers hadn't moved.

The most dangerous thing in consumer research isn't bad data — it's confident people ignoring good data.

What choice modelling actually does

Unlike surveys that ask "do you like this?", choice modelling forces respondents to make trade-offs under realistic constraints — budget, alternatives, shelf context. The output isn't preference scores; it's willingness-to-pay, price elasticity, and simulated market share under different scenarios.

When paired with econometric demand models, you get a powerful view of both stated preferences and revealed behaviour — what people say versus what they actually do with their wallets.

Applying this to revenue management

The real value emerges when you connect these models to pricing decisions. How much can you raise price before volume drops materially? What's the optimal architecture for a portfolio of three pack sizes? Which customer segment is most at risk of switching to a competitor if you discontinue the entry-level SKU?

These aren't hypothetical questions. They're the exact decisions that separate brands growing revenue from brands grinding through margin erosion — and the answers live in well-specified models, not in the gut.
